meta.sourceOfTruth: This file feeds the dashboard island (src/components/Dashboard.jsx). The weekly task edits this file AND writes a new Markdown post in src/content/posts/. Do not hand-edit the dashboard component for data changes.
meta.summary: NVIDIA Dynamo is promoted from the wider field into the main list as the orchestration layer above vLLM/SGLang/TensorRT-LLM; flagship versions reconfirmed unchanged, with a new watchlist entry and a CNCF update for llm-d.

watchlist.name: llm-d
watchlist.note: Kubernetes-native distributed inference (vLLM-based). Accepted into the CNCF Sandbox on Mar 24 2026, backed by IBM, Red Hat, Google, CoreWeave & NVIDIA; now shipping in Red Hat AI Inference on CoreWeave/Azure, with Tesla among early adopters (~3x throughput, ~2x faster TTFT vs round-robin routing).
watchlist.why: Strongest promotion candidate and the K8s-native counterpart to NVIDIA Dynamo — but still at v0.5 with an explicitly unstable API; hold for a stable 1.0 and broader production use before promoting.

watchlist.name: Aphrodite Engine
watchlist.note: High-throughput serving engine built on vLLM's PagedAttention, maintained by the PygmalionAI community; v0.21.0 (May 2 2026), AGPL-3.0. Popular for community and role-play endpoints.
watchlist.why: Active vLLM-derived engine with a dedicated user base; watch whether it differentiates meaningfully from upstream vLLM.

watchlist.name: NanoFlow
watchlist.note: Research-grade engine focused on intra-device parallelism / pipeline overlap; appears in 2026 multi-engine comparisons.
watchlist.why: Promising throughput ideas; verify real-world adoption before promoting.

watchlist.name: DeepSpeed-MII
watchlist.note: Microsoft's serving layer (blocked KV, continuous batching, Dynamic SplitFuse). 2026 benchmarks place it behind SGLang/current vLLM.
watchlist.why: Backed by DeepSpeed; could regain ground with a major release.

usecases.a: Start with vLLM. It's the safest default — the broadest model and hardware coverage (NVIDIA, AMD, TPU, Trainium, Gaudi), the largest contributor base, and the backbone of most Kubernetes serving stacks. You give up ~29% peak throughput versus SGLang/LMDeploy, but you gain the deepest ecosystem and the least chance of hitting an unsupported edge case.

Go SGLang or LMDeploy — both land around ~16,200 tok/s on H100, roughly 29% ahead of vLLM (~12,500). That gap is real money: about $15k/month saved when serving ~1M requests/day. If you're willing to invest in a build/tuning step for the absolute ceiling, TensorRT-LLM goes higher still.

usecases.q: RAG / multi-turn chat (lots of shared prefix)
usecases.a: SGLang, decisively. Its RadixAttention automatically reuses the KV cache for shared prefixes (system prompts, retrieved context, conversation history), delivering up to 6.4x on prefix-heavy traffic. The more your requests overlap, the bigger the win — this is the workload SGLang was built for.

LMDeploy. Its TurboMind engine is the throughput leader for quantized serving — 4-bit inference runs ~2.4x faster than FP16, and it adds TurboQuant KV-cache quantization on top. Especially strong for InternLM/Qwen families and cost-optimized endpoints. Keep it patched — it had a recently-exploited CVE.

usecases.q: Absolute peak performance, NVIDIA-only, willing to tune
usecases.a: TensorRT-LLM has the highest ceiling on NVIDIA hardware, with first-class support for the newest features (NVFP4, Blackwell/B200) and tight Triton Inference Server integration. The trade-off is the steepest learning curve and an engine build/compile step — worth it for latency-critical, well-defined deployments, less so for fast-moving experimentation.

usecases.q: Coordinating inference across many GPUs or nodes (disaggregated serving)
usecases.a: NVIDIA Dynamo. It's an orchestration layer that sits above vLLM, SGLang, or TensorRT-LLM — not a replacement for them. It splits prefill and decode into independently-scaled GPU pools, adds KV-aware routing to skip redundant prefill (~2x faster TTFT), tiers the KV cache from GPU down to CPU/SSD/object storage, and uses an SLA-driven Planner to autoscale at minimum TCO. For a Kubernetes-only, vLLM-centric alternative, llm-d (now in the CNCF Sandbox) is the one to watch. Both are overkill on a single node — reach for them when one box stops being enough.

usecases.q: Run models locally on a laptop / CPU / Mac
usecases.a: Ollama if you want the lowest-friction experience — one command to pull and run, an OpenAI-compatible API, and now a desktop GUI plus cloud fallback for models too big to run locally. Drop down to llama.cpp directly when you want full control, edge hardware (Apple Metal, AMD CDNA4, Qualcomm Hexagon), or to embed inference in your own app — Ollama is built on it.

MLC-LLM / WebLLM. Its compile-once, deploy-anywhere model (via Apache TVM) is unmatched for breadth — run on WebGPU in a browser, on iOS/Android, or on embedded hardware, with no server needed. The compilation workflow is more involved than Ollama, but nothing else covers the browser + mobile space as well for privacy-first, no-backend apps.

usecases.q: Big models on 1–4 consumer GPUs at home
usecases.a: ExLlamaV3 + TabbyAPI. The EXL3 format gives the best quality-per-bit on consumer NVIDIA GPUs, with tensor/expert parallelism so you can spread a large model across a multi-GPU rig. It's also often first to support brand-new quantized releases (it was the only stack for some Qwen3.5 quants), and TabbyAPI gives you an OpenAI-compatible endpoint for personal or small-team use.

Plan a migration. TGI was archived (read-only) in March 2026 and is no longer developed — Hugging Face itself now points users to vLLM or SGLang, both of which adopted the transformers-architecture approach TGI pioneered. Move to vLLM for the safest general-purpose path, or SGLang if throughput is your priority.

engines.name: vLLM
engines.tagline: The most mature, broadest-ecosystem open-source serving engine.
engines.perfNote: ~12,553 tok/s on H100 in 2026 head-to-heads — slower peak than SGLang/LMDeploy but widest coverage.
keyFeatures: Model Runner V2 default for Qwen3 dense; experimental Rust frontend
strengths: Backbone of Kubernetes stacks (KServe, llm-d, Ray Serve)
tradeoffs: Not the fastest on raw H100 throughput
Config surface is large; many knobs to tune
bestUseCases: General-purpose production serving across mixed hardware
Teams wanting the safest, best-supported default
recent: v0.22.1 (Jun 5 2026): 367 commits, 202 contributors
DeepSeek V4 hardening with NVFP4 fused MoE + CUDA graphs
Transformers v4 deprecated; C++20 build requirement

engines.name: SGLang
engines.tagline: Fastest open engine for prefix-heavy and multi-turn workloads.
engines.perfNote: ~16,215 tok/s on H100 — ~29% faster than vLLM, up to 6.4x on prefix-heavy RAG/chat via RadixAttention.
strengths: Strong on newest NVIDIA silicon (GB300, 25x on GB300 NVL72)
tradeoffs: NVIDIA-centric; thinner non-NVIDIA support than vLLM
Younger ecosystem than vLLM
bestUseCases: RAG and multi-turn chat (heavy prefix reuse)
recent: DeepSeek V4, Gemma 4 MTP, Ring-2.6-1T model support
25x inference on NVIDIA GB300 NVL72 (Feb 2026)

engines.name: TensorRT-LLM
engines.tagline: NVIDIA's max-performance engine for NVIDIA GPUs.
hardware: NVIDIA only (B200, Blackwell, Hopper, RTX 50 via WSL)
engines.perfNote: Highest achievable throughput/latency on NVIDIA when fully tuned — at the cost of build complexity.
keyFeatures: Custom attention kernels, in-flight batching, paged KV
FP8 / FP4 / NVFP4 / INT4-AWQ / INT8-SmoothQuant quantization
Helix Parallelism, Sparse Attention, Torch Compile + piecewise CUDA graph
PyTorch-architected high-level LLM API + C++/Python runtimes
strengths: Peak performance ceiling on NVIDIA hardware
First-class on newest NVIDIA features (NVFP4, Blackwell)
tradeoffs: NVIDIA-only, steepest learning curve
Engine build/compile step adds friction vs. vLLM/SGLang
bestUseCases: Squeezing maximum performance from NVIDIA fleets
recent: Base image to PyTorch 25.12, Torch 2.9.1
B200 support; NVFP4 GEMM for Llama/Mixtral
New guides: Helix Parallelism, Sparse Attention, Ray Orchestrator

engines.name: LMDeploy
engines.tagline: Throughput leader for quantized model serving (TurboMind).
engines.perfNote: ~16,132 tok/s on H100 (≈ SGLang); 4-bit inference ~2.4x faster than FP16; up to 1.8x vLLM request throughput.
keyFeatures: TurboMind engine, continuous batching, blocked KV cache
Weight-only + K/V quantization, TurboQuant KV cache quant
tradeoffs: Smaller ecosystem / community than vLLM
Had a recently-exploited CVE — keep patched
Documentation thinner in English
recent: Qwen3.5 + llm-compressor 4-bit quant, TurboQuant KV cache
Anthropic-compatible endpoints; Ascend Qwen3.5 support

engines.name: NVIDIA Dynamo
engines.tagline: Datacenter-scale orchestration that turns vLLM/SGLang/TensorRT-LLM into one coordinated multi-node system.
engines.perfNote: An orchestration layer, not a kernel engine: ~2x faster TTFT via KV-aware routing, ~7x throughput/GPU and ~7x faster cold-starts; up to 750x throughput on DeepSeek-R1 (GB300 NVL72) vs prior-gen baselines.
keyFeatures: Disaggregated prefill/decode across independently-scaled GPU pools
KV-aware routing (NATS-coordinated) to skip redundant prefill
KV Block Manager: GPU → CPU → SSD → remote/S3/Azure KV offload
ModelExpress weight streaming over NIXL/NVLink for fast cold-starts
SLA-driven Planner autoscaler + Grove K8s gang scheduling
strengths: Purpose-built for multi-node / disaggregated datacenter serving
NVIDIA-backed with broad adoption (Baseten, Mistral, Moonshot, Dell, WEKA)
Zero-config DGDR deploy plus agentic, multimodal and video features in 1.0
tradeoffs: An orchestration layer, not a standalone engine — it needs a backend
NVIDIA-centric; complexity only pays off at multi-GPU/multi-node scale
Overkill for single-GPU or single-node serving
bestUseCases: Coordinating LLM serving across many GPUs/nodes
SLA-driven autoscaling at minimum TCO on Kubernetes
recent: v1.3.x dev builds: model-specific recipes for DeepSeek-V4, Nemotron-3 Ultra/Super, Kimi K2.6 on vLLM + TRT-LLM backends
v1.1.1 (May 9 2026); 1.0 GA at GTC; zero-config DGDR deploy, multimodal E/P/D, storage-tier KV offload

engines.name: llama.cpp
engines.tagline: Portable C/C++ inference everywhere — CPU, Apple Silicon, any GPU.
engines.perfNote: April 2026 rewrite: ~2.1x throughput on 70B quantized, ~1.4x on 7B; GGUF format.
keyFeatures: Largest rewrite since 2023: new kernel generator, contiguous per-head KV, unified backend dispatch
MTP speculative decoding; DeepSeek V4 native FP4/FP8
strengths: Best CPU and Apple Silicon story
Foundation for Ollama, LM Studio and many local apps
tradeoffs: Lower peak datacenter throughput than vLLM/SGLang
Manual setup vs. turnkey wrappers
recent: Build b9631 (Jun 14 2026) + llama.app website launched for one-click local AI
Tensor parallelism (b8738) + Qualcomm Hexagon NPU backend (b8755)
MTP speculative decoding + DeepSeek V4 native FP4/FP8 (May 2026)

engines.name: Ollama
engines.tagline: The easiest way to run models locally — now with a desktop GUI and cloud.
engines.perfNote: Wraps llama.cpp + MLX runners; optimized for convenience over peak throughput.
keyFeatures: One-command model pull & run, OpenAI-compatible API
Built-in Windows/desktop GUI (system tray, chat history, model switch)
Cloud integration for large models (e.g. GPT-OSS 120B) with auto local/cloud switching
Improved scheduler reducing OOM crashes; faster MLX sampling
tradeoffs: Not built for high-concurrency production serving
Abstraction hides tuning knobs power users want
bestUseCases: Quick model trials before committing to a server engine
recent: Gemma 4 MTP spec decoding on Macs (2x+ speedup for Gemma 4 31B); Gemma 4 QAT + Nemotron-3-Ultra
Metal GPU offload for multimodal on Apple Silicon; load-stall detection; Qwen Code / Cline CLI

engines.name: MLC-LLM
engines.tagline: Compile-once, deploy-anywhere — mobile, browser, desktop, edge.
engines.perfNote: TVM/ML-compilation backend generates optimized kernels per target device.
strengths: No server needed for on-device/private inference
Single workflow across very different targets
tradeoffs: Compilation workflow is more involved
Smaller community; less turnkey than Ollama
recent: Ongoing model library refreshes
Continued WebLLM + Snapdragon GPU support

engines.name: ExLlamaV3 + TabbyAPI
engines.perfNote: EXL3 format targets max quality-per-bit on consumer GPUs; sometimes the only stack for newest quants (e.g. Qwen3.5).
keyFeatures: EXL3 quantization format (high quality at low bits)
Dynamic batching, speculative decoding, cache quantization
OpenAI-compatible server via TabbyAPI (HF download, embeddings, Jinja2 templates)
strengths: Early support for cutting-edge quantized models
Lightweight and fast for single-/few-user setups
tradeoffs: Consumer-NVIDIA focused; not a datacenter scale-out engine
Smaller community; enthusiast-oriented
bestUseCases: Enthusiasts running big models on 1-4 consumer GPUs
Running the newest quantized releases early
Local OpenAI-compatible endpoint for personal/team use
recent: Only supported stack for some Qwen3.5 quants (Mar 2026)
EXL3 tensor/expert parallel + cache quantization

engines.name: TGI (Text Generation Inference)
engines.tagline: Hugging Face's former serving engine — now archived.
engines.perfNote: Repo archived & read-only since Mar 21 2026. HF now recommends vLLM / SGLang.
keyFeatures: Final release 3.3.5 added ROCm, Torch 2.7, CUDA 12.8
strengths: Still powers some HF Inference Endpoints
tradeoffs: No longer developed — avoid for new projects
HF itself points users to vLLM/SGLang
bestUseCases: Migrate to vLLM or SGLang going forward
recent: Repository archived March 21 2026 (read-only)
HF moved to a transformers-architecture strategy adopted by vLLM/SGLang
